citation: Labbe A, Foret W, Troy M, Page P, Savoie F "Buddy." Kinematic Sequencing of the Football Pass Using Inertial Motion Analysis. Int J Sports Phys Ther. 2025;20(8):1214-1221.
abstract: This pilot descriptive cross-sectional study used wireless Noraxon Ultium IMUs (16 sensors) on 8 right-handed quarterbacks (4 high school, 4 collegiate; mean age 16.4 years) performing 9 drop-back passes at 10, 20, and 30 yards. Kinematic data were analyzed at four points of interest — foot contact, maximal external rotation (MaxER), ball release, and maximal internal rotation — using Noraxon MyoMotion 3.18 software. Shoulder abduction and external rotation peaked at 112° and 134° at MaxER, elbow flexion peaked at 100°, and minimal hip-shoulder separation (11°) occurred at ball release, with trunk variables converging to near-neutral just before release.
tldr: This pilot study provides the first IMU-based kinematic sequence data for football quarterbacks, showing that lower body posture remains relatively stable throughout the throw while the trunk shifts away from the throwing side and converges to near-neutral at ball release. Hip-shoulder separation and trunk rotation patterns mirror key principles from baseball pitching research but differ in elbow extension angle and trunk involvement. For QB development staff, these normative angles provide a baseline for pre-season assessment, injury risk monitoring, and rehabilitation benchmarks — particularly for shoulder ER range, lumbar extension load during cocking, and the importance of coordinated hip-trunk sequencing for power generation.
methods: Pilot descriptive cross-sectional study. Eight healthy, right-handed, pain-free quarterbacks (4 high school, 4 collegiate) with at least 2 years of experience performed 3 drop-back passes at each of 3 distances (10, 20, 30 yards) for 9 passes per participant. Sixteen Noraxon Ultium IMUs were placed on lower extremities, trunk, and upper extremities. Kinematic sequence algorithm in Noraxon MyoMotion 3.18 identified joint angles at four points of interest. Mean angles were calculated for shoulder abduction/ER, elbow flexion, knee flexion, trunk inclination/rotation, lumbar extension, pelvis rotation, and hip-shoulder separation.
findings: Shoulder abduction and ER peaked at 112° and 134° at MaxER; elbow flexion peaked at 100°. Shoulder ER at ball release was 56°, elbow extended from 99° to 31°. Stride knee flexion averaged 29°–46°, peaking at ball release. Trunk averaged 23° lumbar extension at MaxER. Trunk rotation began at 40° rightward at foot contact and reversed to 21° leftward at MaxER. Hip-shoulder separation maintained ~20° in cocking phase, quickly reversed to 20° favoring left trunk in follow-through, with only 11° separation at ball release. All trunk measures converged near neutral just before ball release.
limitations: Small sample (n=8) of only right-handed high school and collegiate QBs limits generalizability. Throwing distances were not analyzed separately. IMU limitations include potential skin artifact, electromagnetic interference, and signal drift. IMU data is not directly comparable to gold-standard optical motion capture. The controlled setting cannot replicate real-game spontaneous throwing mechanics.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine professionals can use these normative kinematic angles as baselines for pre-season QB assessments. High shoulder ER (~134°) underscores the need for shoulder flexibility and stability training. Sequencing shoulder strengthening with opposite hip activation through the kinetic chain facilitates hip-shoulder separation. Monitoring hip-shoulder separation and changes in trunk rotation patterns can inform both performance enhancement and injury prevention strategies throughout the season. IMU technology enables repeated field-based assessments without lab constraints.
athleteDev: For QB development, this study establishes field-accessible kinematic benchmarks: ~112° shoulder abduction, ~134° ER at MaxER, and ~46° peak knee flexion. Coaches can use IMU assessments to evaluate throwing mechanics at multiple distances during pre-season. Trunk rotation of ~60° total in the transverse plane and hip-shoulder separation principles should guide QB-specific strength and mobility programming. Lumbar extension demands (~23°) during cocking highlight the importance of core and thoracic mobility in QB training.
rtp: For QBs returning from shoulder or elbow injuries, these normative IMU-derived angles provide reference targets: ~134° shoulder ER, ~100° elbow flexion at cocking, and ~56° shoulder ER at ball release. Progressive achievement of these kinematic benchmarks can serve as objective milestones in return-to-throw protocols. The study's pre-season assessment model supports using IMU-based kinematic profiling to establish individual baselines for comparing post-injury mechanics before clearing for full competition.

citation: Josse CM, Morin J-B, Clark KP, Greenshields JT, Wellman AD. Kinetics and kinematics of initial sprint acceleration performance in Division I collegiate American football players. J Sports Sci. 2025. doi:10.1080/02640414.2025.2567779.
abstract: This pilot study collected the first direct measurements of kinetic and kinematic variables associated with initial sprint acceleration in 123 male NCAA Division I football players categorized into Skill (DB, WR, RB), Big-Skill (TE, OLB, ILB, QB), and Lineman (OL, DL) groups. Players performed maximal sprints of 20–30 m from a three-point stance; kinetics were derived from a linear motorized encoder and force-velocity-power (FVP) profiles were calculated. Kinematics were derived from slow-motion video at 240 Hz analyzed in Kinovea. Maximal power (Pmax) had the largest relationship with 0–10 m time across all position groups (r = −0.60 to −0.90), while key kinematic variables at toe-off and touchdown also showed large significant relationships with Pmax.
tldr: This is the first study to provide direct kinetic and kinematic sprint acceleration data in Division I football players by position group. Pmax is the single strongest predictor of 0–10 m acceleration across all positions, making it a priority training target. Skill players are fastest, linemen slowest, with distinct mechanical profiles: linemen have longer contact times, smaller flight lengths, and less favorable limb angles. Practically, performance staff should prioritize increasing Pmax and optimizing FVP profile orientation for position-specific sprint distances, and use kinematic feedback (swing leg hip/knee flexion at toe-off, shank angles at touchdown) to identify and correct sprint mechanics that limit acceleration.
methods: Pilot study with 128 male NCAA Division I players (age 21±2 yr; mass 101.5±20.0 kg) performing 1–2 maximal sprints of 20–30 m from a three-point stance on indoor artificial turf. Kinetics from 1080 Sprint linear encoder (333 Hz) in isotonic mode; FVP profiles calculated per validated field method (Samozino et al.). Kinematics from 240 Hz slow-motion video analyzed in Kinovea with 18-point manual digitization; first 3 steps including initial push-off analyzed. ICC = 0.96 for sprint trial reliability; ICC = 0.96±0.03 for kinematic marker reliability. Groups: Skill (n=62), Big-Skill (n=37), Lineman (n=24). ANOVAs with Bonferroni post-hoc and Pearson correlations used.
findings: Pmax (W/kg) had large-to-nearly-perfect relationships with 0–10 m time for all groups (r = −0.60 to −0.90, p<0.001). Skill group: Pmax 18.6±1.4 W/kg, 10 m time 1.93±0.07 s. Big-Skill: 17.0±1.5 W/kg, 2.00±0.07 s. Lineman: 14.3±1.6 W/kg, 2.14±0.08 s. F0 had large-to-very-large relationships for Lineman (r=−0.80) and Big-Skill (r=−0.65) but not Skill (r=−0.27). RFmax only significantly related to Lineman 0–10 m (r=−0.72). Spatiotemporally, contact time (r=−0.69) and flight length (r=0.56) had large correlations with Pmax. At toe-off, swing leg knee angle (r=−0.67), swing leg hip angle (r=−0.59), and CM-toe distance (r=−0.55) had large correlations. At touchdown, stance and swing shank angles (r=−0.57 each) and stance knee angle (r=−0.51) had large correlations.
limitations: Kinematic-FVP relationships reported only across the whole population, not at inter-group level. Lineman group was relatively small (n=24). Data from Division I collegiate players may not generalize to NFL populations. Training interventions were not identical across two testing cohorts. Some recordings were captured at 120 Hz rather than 240 Hz due to IOS software issues. No analysis of intra-group or intra-positional fastest vs. slowest performers.
practicalImplications: Pmax is the primary kinetic KPI for initial acceleration monitoring in all football players. Training should prioritize increasing Pmax, with secondary attention to FVP profile orientation appropriate for position-specific sprint distances. For linemen (≥106 kg), F0 and mechanical effectiveness (RFmax) become increasingly important. Coaches and S&C staff should use sprint encoder profiling alongside video-based kinematic assessment to identify swing leg mechanics (hip/knee flexion at toe-off) and landing shank angles (at touchdown) as technique targets for acceleration improvement.
athleteDev: Position-specific normative kinetic and kinematic data from this study allow football performance staff to benchmark 0–10 m sprint acceleration for Skill, Big-Skill, and Lineman groups. Sprint FVP profiling with a linear encoder is now feasible as a standard off-season testing tool to monitor Pmax development. Linemen require force-oriented training while skill players benefit from velocity-oriented approaches. Kinematic cues — greater swing leg hip/knee flexion at toe-off, toe further behind CM, and reduced shank angles at touchdown — are measurable technical targets for acceleration phase coaching.
rtp: This study's FVP profile and kinematic normative data can serve as return-to-sprint benchmarks for football players recovering from lower extremity injuries. Pmax deficits relative to pre-injury or position-group norms provide an objective threshold before clearing for full-speed sprint work. Contact time prolongation and flight length reduction (common compensations post-injury) can be monitored via linear encoder testing. Kinematic asymmetries at toe-off and touchdown can identify limb-loading hesitancy before return to practice.

citation: Andriolo L, Marín Fermín T, Gaggia GMMC, Serner A, Kon E, Papakostas E, Massey A, Verdonk P, Filardo G. Knee Cartilage Injuries in Football Players: Clinical Outcomes and Return to Sport After Surgical Treatment: A Systematic Review of the Literature. Cartilage. 2024;1-15.
abstract: This systematic review analyzed clinical outcomes and return-to-sport after surgical management of knee cartilage lesions in football (soccer) players. Fifteen studies on 409 football players (86% men, 14% women, mean age 26.3 years) were included from PubMed, Web of Science, and Cochrane Library searches through August 2023. Surgical techniques studied included arthroscopic debridement, bone marrow stimulation (BMS), scaffold-augmented BMS, osteochondral autograft transplantation (OAT/mosaicplasty), autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI), and matrix-assisted autologous chondrocyte transplantation (MACT). Lesion size influenced treatment choice, with debridement used for smallest lesions (~1.1 cm²) and ACI for largest (~5.8 cm²). Fastest recovery was seen with debridement and scaffold-augmented BMS (~2.5–3 months); OAT averaged 6.1 months and MACT 10.3 months to return to sport.
tldr: This systematic review synthesizes the best available evidence on surgical treatment of knee cartilage injuries in competitive football players. The key message for sports medicine staff is that treatment choice is driven by lesion size, and microfractures (BMS) — while most commonly used and fastest to RTS at ~4.6 months — show clinical deterioration over time. Scaffold-augmented BMS and OAT offer better durability, while MACT provides more stable long-term outcomes but requires ~10 months recovery. Decision-making in elite athletes must balance early return-to-sport against long-term joint health. Staff should set realistic expectations: overall RTS to previous level ranges from 33% (ACI, large lesions) to 100% (debridement/scaffold-BMS, small lesions), and combined ACL/cartilage lesions carry a 5-fold increased OA risk.
methods: Systematic literature review per PRISMA guidelines using PubMed, Web of Science, and Cochrane Library (search August 7, 2023). Included studies with football player populations, knee cartilage injuries, and surgical management. Methodological quality assessed with modified Coleman Methodology Score (CMS); risk of bias assessed with RoB2 (RCT) and RoBANS2 (non-randomized studies). Fifteen studies included: 9 prospective case series, 2 retrospective case series, 1 RCT, 1 prospective comparative study, 1 case report, 1 multicenter survey (N=409 football players total).
findings: BMS (n=139): RTS to same level 81%, mean 4.6 months; clinical scores improve but deteriorate over time. Scaffold-augmented BMS (n=36): 100% RTS at same level, 2.5–3 months; short follow-up only. OAT/mosaicplasty (n=122): 67% RTS to same level, 6.1 months; RCT showed OAT superior to microfractures (93% vs 52% RTS). ACI first generation (n=49): 33% RTS, 18.1 months; used for large lesions (~5.8 cm²). MACT (n=48): 69% RTS to same level, 10.3 months; more durable long-term vs BMS. Mean modified CMS of included studies was 43.5/100, indicating low methodological quality with high risk of bias overall.
limitations: Low methodological quality (mean CMS 43.5/100) and high risk of bias across studies. Many studies mix football players with broader athlete populations. Region-based patterns in surgical technique use introduce confounding. No comparative studies exist for OAT vs. MACT in professional football players specifically. Most ACI data is from older first-generation techniques. Evidence is insufficient for definitive consensus guidelines.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine staff managing football players with knee cartilage injuries should expect: debridement suitable for small lesions with fastest RTS (~2.5 months) but risk of progression; BMS most commonly used with fastest RTS among repair techniques (~4.6 months) but potential for long-term deterioration; OAT/mosaicplasty offering better durability for lesions ~2.2 cm² with ~6 months RTS; MACT best long-term results but longest recovery (~10 months). Lesion size, subchondral involvement, and athlete age/level should guide surgical decision-making. Combined ACL and cartilage lesions dramatically increase OA risk and warrant careful long-term monitoring.
athleteDev: American football players with knee cartilage injuries represent a similar population to soccer players in terms of high-impact loading demands. This review's framework — lesion-size-guided treatment selection and RTS timelines — is directly applicable to football athlete management. BMS (microfractures) is the fastest path back but may compromise long-term joint health in young football players who face many more competitive seasons. For development-stage athletes (college and early professional), scaffold-augmented BMS or OAT may be preferable to prioritize durability over the earliest possible RTS.
rtp: Return-to-sport timelines from this review provide benchmarks for football RTP planning: debridement ~2.5 months; scaffold-augmented BMS ~2.5–3 months; BMS/microfractures ~4.6 months; OAT/mosaicplasty ~6.1 months; MACT ~10.3 months; ACI ~18 months. Key RTP predictors include shorter preoperative symptom duration, younger age, and smaller lesion size. Younger patients and those with trauma-related (vs. degenerative) lesions have better RTS outcomes. Previous surgery is the strongest negative predictor for returning to prior performance level. Football staff should monitor for long-term BMS deterioration and plan re-evaluation imaging at 1–2 years post-procedure.

citation: Hernandez RJ, Lamplot JD, Hammond KE, Joshi NB, Wong PK, Umpirrez M, Singer A, Gonzalez FM. Knee Injuries in the Elite American Football Player: A Descriptive Pictorial Imaging and Mechanism of Injury Review. J Comput Assist Tomogr. 2022;00:00-00.
abstract: This narrative review article, supported by illustrative MRI and radiograph cases from Emory University, describes the most common knee injuries in elite American football players and the imaging findings used to diagnose them. Injury types covered include MCL sprains (Grades 1–3 including distal Stener lesions and ramp lesions), medial and lateral meniscal tears (bucket handle, radial, root, Wrisberg rip), ACL tears and secondary signs, PCL tears, posterolateral corner (PLC) injuries, posteromedial corner (PMC) injuries, extensor mechanism ruptures, IT band syndrome, bone contusion patterns (pivot-shift, dashboard, hyperextension, clip, patellar dislocation), and patellar instability. The review synthesizes epidemiology, MRI characteristics, and treatment frameworks for each injury type.
tldr: This comprehensive pictorial review is an essential reference for sports medicine staff evaluating knee MRI in American football players. The knee is the most commonly injured joint in football (75% of injuries during games), and this paper provides a systematic imaging-based framework for each major injury type. Key highlights include: MCL injury in 78% of knee sprains; lateral meniscus injury twice as common as medial in NFL Combine athletes; 10% of NFL Combine players report prior ACL tear; articular cartilage defects in up to 60% of NFL prospective players; PCL injury more common on artificial turf; patellar instability ~3% of knee injuries. Understanding characteristic bone contusion patterns (pivot shift, clip, hyperextension, dashboard) allows radiologists and clinicians to predict ligamentous injury from MRI alone.
methods: Narrative/descriptive review article with pictorial case examples. Literature synthesis from published epidemiology, imaging studies, and clinical outcome data in NFL and collegiate football players. Imaging examples sourced from Emory University's musculoskeletal radiology department. Injuries reviewed: MCL (including ramp lesions), meniscal pathology, ACL, PCL, PLC, PMC, extensor mechanism, IT band, bone contusions, and patellar instability. MRI sequences and findings described for each injury type. Relevant radiograph findings and grading systems summarized.
findings: MCL injured in 78% of knee sprains at NFL training camp; distal sMCL tears (5% of MCL injuries) and Stener lesions have poorer healing. Lateral meniscus tears occurred twice as often as medial (15.6% vs 7.8%) in NFL Combine athletes; prior partial meniscectomy associated with up to 27% prevalence of radiographic knee OA. ACL tear reported in 10% of NFL Combine athletes; noncontact mechanism now considered more common in professional football. PCL injury in 3% of NCAA football players; 67% more common on artificial turf. PLC injury in 1% of NFL Combine athletes; 70% treated surgically. Articular cartilage defects in up to 60% of NFL Combine players; most asymptomatic. Patellar instability in ~3% of knee injuries; cartilage injury in up to 95% of first-time dislocations. QT ruptures return-to-play 50% vs 80% for patellar tendon ruptures.
limitations: Narrative review without systematic search or meta-analysis; no pooled outcome data. Imaging examples reflect a single institution's case archive. Generalizability limited to elite (NFL/college) football players. Epidemiological data cited from varying study designs and time periods. MRI sensitivity for ramp lesions remains less than 50%, emphasizing clinical suspicion over imaging alone.
practicalImplications: Football sports medicine staff should use the bone contusion 'footprints' described in this review to predict associated ligamentous injuries from MRI: pivot-shift pattern (posterior lateral tibial plateau + midlateral femoral condyle) should trigger PLC evaluation; clip injuries suggest MCL damage; hyperextension patterns may involve both ACL and PCL. Ramp lesion incidence of 40% with ACL injury warrants high clinical suspicion even with negative MRI. Meniscal preservation should be prioritized given the 27% OA prevalence with prior meniscectomy. Cartilage defects in NFL players are common but often asymptomatic, requiring careful decision-making about intervention timing.
athleteDev: American football athlete development staff should be aware that knee injuries increase proportionally with competition level — emphasizing the importance of neuromuscular training, landing mechanics, and lower extremity strengthening programs throughout development. The high prevalence of asymptomatic cartilage defects (up to 60% at NFL Combine) suggests that routine pre-participation MRI screening in elite football players may reveal subclinical pathology that requires monitoring over time. Extensor mechanism health (pre-season ultrasound screening showing 80% of NCAA players have prior pathology) correlates with future injury risk and should be incorporated into athlete monitoring programs.
rtp: This review provides essential imaging criteria for RTP decision-making in football players. Grade 1–2 MCL injuries are managed conservatively with progressive rehab; Grade 3 or Stener lesions may require surgical intervention before safe return. Meniscal repair RTP is slower than meniscectomy but associated with lower long-term OA risk. ACL RTP protocols should include evaluation for ramp lesions and concomitant PLC/PMC injuries that affect reconstruction stability. PCL injuries managed surgically (15%) have extended timelines. Cartilage defects requiring OAT are generally season-ending, while chondroplasty allows return within weeks. Patellar instability RTP requires restoration of full ROM and quadriceps strength before return.

citation: Ruh E, Kallman T, Lyden E, Greiner J. Knee injury mechanism varies by injury category: Video analysis of multi-ligament knee injuries in the National Football League. Knee Surg Sports Traumatol Arthrosc. 2025;1-8.
abstract: This retrospective video analysis examined 35 multi-ligament knee injuries (MLKIs) from official NFL game footage spanning 1997–2022, identified from publicly available injury surveillance data. Athletes were categorized by ligament injury pattern (Category I: ACL+MCL; IIa: ACL+LCL; IIb: ACL+PCL; IIIa/IIIb: three-ligament injuries). A standardized 9-question questionnaire assessed hip, knee, and ankle position at time of injury. The most common MLKI was ACL+MCL (65%) and occurred predominantly from direct contact (80%). Statistically significant differences in contact direction, knee valgus/varus, knee rotation, knee flexion/extension, and hip rotation were found between injury categories, confirming that injury mechanism varies meaningfully by MLKI type.
tldr: This is the first study to characterize multi-ligament knee injury (MLKI) mechanisms specifically in NFL athletes using video analysis. The key finding is that 80% of MLKIs result from direct contact — contrasting with isolated ACL injuries which are predominantly non-contact or indirect. ACL+MCL injuries (65% of MLKIs) occur from lateral direct contact causing valgus/external rotation on a flexed knee with hip internal rotation — closely resembling isolated ACL injury mechanics. ACL+LCL and ACL+PCL injuries occur from varus/internal rotation on an extended knee, and three-ligament injuries exclusively from direct anterolateral contact. Offensive skill players (QBs and WRs, 17.5% each) are disproportionately affected during ball-carrying plays, with 57% of MLKIs occurring while the player was the ball carrier. Football sports medicine staff must plan for position-specific MLKI risks and be prepared for the substantially worse RTP outcomes with non-MCL MLKIs.
methods: Retrospective video analysis of 40 identified MLKIs from NFL injury surveillance data (1997–2022), confirmed via team reports and ESPN articles. Videos obtained from NFL+ streaming service; 35 cases had analyzable footage (5 excluded — practice injuries). A 9-question questionnaire assessed contact type (direct/indirect/noncontact), direction of contact, and hip/knee/ankle position (flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, internal/external rotation) at time of injury. Two raters (orthopaedic surgeon with sports medicine fellowship and medical student) analyzed videos independently; consensus achieved on all disagreements. Fisher's exact test used for categorical comparisons between MLKI groups.
findings: 35 MLKIs analyzed: 65% Category I (ACL+MCL), 15% Category IIa (ACL+LCL), 5% Category IIb (ACL+PCL), 7.5% each Category IIIa/IIIb. Direct contact: 80%; indirect: 14.3%; noncontact: 5.7%. Lateral/posterolateral force most common (67.9%). Overall: valgus in 85%, external tibial rotation in 73.5%, knee flexion in 70.6%. Category I: 100% valgus, 91.3% external tibial rotation, 82.6% knee flexion, 95.7% hip internal rotation; lateral/posterolateral contact in 84.2%. Category IIa/IIb: 66.7% varus, 66.7% internal tibial rotation, 83.3% knee extension. Category III: 80% valgus, 60% internal tibial rotation, 80% knee flexion; 100% direct contact; all three-ligament injuries. Significant differences across groups: knee valgus/varus (p<0.001), knee rotation (p=0.002), knee flexion/extension (p=0.009), hip rotation (p=0.009).
limitations: No centralized verified injury database; reliance on publicly available surveillance data may under-capture lower-profile injuries and overrepresent catastrophic direct-contact events. Ligament injury grade and degree of laxity could not be confirmed without imaging. Video quality was variable, especially pre-2014. Exact timing of ligament rupture within the dynamic injury event cannot be determined. Cohort size is limited for rarer injury categories (ACL+PCL, three-ligament injuries). Study does not evaluate exposure rates by surface type.
practicalImplications: Football sports medicine staff should recognize that MLKIs are predominantly direct-contact injuries (80%), particularly during ball-carrying plays targeting offensive skill positions. Unlike isolated ACL injury, prevention strategies based on neuromuscular training for noncontact mechanisms are less applicable to MLKI. RTP prognosis varies markedly: ACL+MCL averages 305 days, 70.8% RTP rate, 43.5% returning to prior performance level; ACL+LCL or ACL+PCL averages 459 days, 55.6% RTP, only 18.5% at prior level; three-ligament MLKI averages 609 days with no players returning to prior level. These data should directly inform pre-injury counseling, contract decisions, and post-injury career planning conversations.
athleteDev: MLKI risk is highest for offensive skill positions (QB and WR each at 17.5%) during high-velocity direct-contact ball-carrying plays, suggesting that targeted protective rule enforcement and practice protocols may reduce exposure for these positions. Training staff should include MLKI education and mechanism awareness in pre-season educational programming. The finding that 97.1% of affected athletes were not wearing knee braces raises questions about prophylactic bracing for high-risk positions, though evidence on brace efficacy for MLKI prevention is limited.
rtp: MLKI RTP outcomes from this study's referenced data provide essential benchmarks: ACL+MCL injuries average 305 days to RTP with 70.8% return rate and 43.5% returning to original performance level — the best outcomes of the MLKI categories. ACL+LCL or ACL+PCL injuries average 459 days, 55.6% RTP, 18.5% at prior level. Three-ligament injuries average 609 days with 0% returning to prior performance. Staff should establish RTP milestones calibrated to injury category severity. The biomechanical differences between categories (valgus/flexion for ACL+MCL vs. varus/extension for ACL+PCL and ACL+LCL) may also inform rehabilitation emphasis — restoring valgus and rotational stability being paramount for ACL+MCL injuries.
